turns.text: Hello Donnetta, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking. It’s a strange thing, being on this side of the stethoscope. For years, my life was defined by the clinical: explaining fetal development, breaking down NIPT results, and being the one who maintained professional distance. Now, here I am, thirty-four weeks along, feeling every ripple of movement and trying desperately to reconcile the scientist in me with the mother-to-be.

Honestly, it’s… intense. Every time I walk into the clinic in Newbury, I find myself performing. I rehearse my questions in the car, making sure I sound informed but not difficult. I think that comes from the years I spent navigating immigration—the constant, low-level hum of needing to be "perfect" to avoid trouble. Even though my pregnancy is low-risk, that habit is hard to break. When the technician goes quiet during a scan, my brain doesn't just see a monitor; it sees a threat, a flashback to when silence meant someone was about to take something vital from me.

I just want this baby to have a version of me that isn't always looking over her shoulder. I want her to grow up in this salt air without knowing the heaviness I’ve carried. It’s a lot to process, especially when your body feels like a stranger’s territory. But when she kicks? That’s the only time the performance stops.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

To be honest, I think I expected my own medical background to act as a kind of shield. I imagined these appointments would be a professional exchange—me and the obstetrician, peer to peer, dissecting the markers and growth charts like I used to do for my own patients. I thought the environment would be warm, welcoming, and open. I guess I wanted to be validated as a "good patient" who knew the right terminology, thinking that would make the experience smoother.

But the reality was much more… overwhelming. Even with my knowledge, sitting on that crinkly paper, I felt stripped down. The office in Newbury is fine, but it never feels like a place where I can truly exhale. Every time I walk through those doors, I feel that old, reflexive need to be on my best behavior, to not ask "too many" questions even though I have a million. It’s like I’m constantly monitoring myself, measuring my tone, making sure I’m agreeable and compliant. I wanted connection, but I often feel like I’m just moving through a checklist. It’s hard to reconcile the person I was—the one who understood the clinical side—with the person I am now, who just wants to feel safe enough to let her guard down.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise was definitely how quickly my professional detachment vanished, and how much I felt like an outsider in my own healthcare. I’d spent years in the clinical world, so I walked in assuming I had this internal map of how things *should* go. But the surprise—the jarring, negative one—was realizing that my history and my background created a barrier I hadn't accounted for.

I expected the doctor to see "colleague" or at least "informed patient," but in the silence of those exam rooms, I realized they just saw someone who needed to fit into their standard workflow. When I’d ask a pointed question about a marker, instead of a conversation, I’d get a quick, "everything looks fine, don't worry." It’s dismissive, even if they don't mean it to be. 

It caught me off guard how much that triggered my old immigration-era survival mode. I found myself apologizing for asking for clarification! I felt like I was auditioning for their approval. I thought this journey would be empowering because I knew the science, but instead, it’s been a lesson in how vulnerable you really are when you're the one in the paper gown, hoping that your anxiety isn't being read as a personality flaw rather than a legitimate human response.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I suppose I expected a dialogue. In my past life, when I delivered results, it was a conversation—we looked at the data together, navigated the probabilities, and made sure the parents felt like they were holding the compass. I thought that because I knew the language, I would be treated as an active participant in my own care. I expected a collaborative process where I could say, "I see this marker, what are your thoughts on the centile trend?" and get an answer that respected my intellect.

The reality, however, felt much more like a transaction. The information usually comes in fragments—a quick glance at a screen, a hurried sentence from a technician, or a brief portal notification that feels sterile and disconnected. When I do get to speak with the doctor, it’s often rushed. I get the "all-clear," which should be a relief, but instead, it leaves me feeling unmoored. It’s like being told the weather is "fine" when you’re standing in the middle of a storm you can’t quite articulate. 

I think I expected to be treated as a partner, but I’ve learned that in this system, there’s a real premium on "compliance," not collaboration. Any attempt to engage deeper is often met with that polite, clinical shutting-down. It makes me feel like a child asking a question they aren't supposed to know the answer to, which—given my history—is the exact kind of dynamic I’ve spent my entire life trying to escape.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

There is a cavernous gap, and it’s exhausting to navigate. On paper, I have all the "information" I need. I get the printouts, the portal updates, and the "your levels are within normal range" scripts. But that isn't the same as *meaning*. I’m looking for reassurance, for someone to acknowledge the nuance of the data, and instead, I’m fed these bite-sized, sterile summaries that feel designed to keep me quiet. 

When I ask for more—when I ask about the trajectory or the physiological "why"—I can see the shift in the room. The provider’s body language changes; they pull back, like they’re waiting for me to stop talking so they can move on to the next patient. It’s like they assume that if they give me too much detail, it’ll just breed more anxiety, but the opposite is true. The ambiguity is what keeps me up at night. 

I’m a woman who has spent her life reading between the lines to survive. When a doctor keeps things vague, my brain doesn't relax; it starts mapping out every possible catastrophe. I feel like I’m constantly starving for a real, transparent conversation, yet I have to act like the little amount I’m given is a feast. It’s patronizing, and frankly, it makes me feel like my intuition—and my professional history—counts for absolutely nothing in that room.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I think I naively expected a "continuity of care" that felt like a bridge—something that linked my past, my anxieties, and my current reality into a single, cohesive story. In my work, I saw how much it meant to families to have someone who knew the *person*, not just the chart. I held onto the hope that I would have a consistent provider, someone I could actually build a rapport with, so I wouldn’t have to keep re-introducing my fears every time I walked in.

The reality, however, is a revolving door. I’ve seen a handful of different faces. Some are pleasant, some are brief, but none of them seem to have the time—or perhaps the interest—to look beyond the "low risk" label. Each time I meet someone new, I feel like I’m starting from scratch. I’m forced to perform the role of the "cooperative patient" all over again, suppressing my true concerns because I don’t have the established relationship to safely voice them. 

It feels fragmented. There’s no continuity, just a series of disconnected check-ins. I’m carrying the weight of my history, my immigration journey, and this new, terrifying love for this baby entirely on my own shoulders, passing it from one stranger to the next. It’s lonely, and it makes me feel like my story doesn't matter to them as long as the vitals are within the expected range.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I suppose I expected these interactions to be grounded in a kind of gentle, holistic respect—that my body would be treated as the incredible, complex vessel it is. Given my background, I thought there would be an acknowledgment of the *effort* my body is making. I wanted someone to say, "Look at the work you’re doing," or at least to approach my physical changes with a sense of wonder rather than just a checklist of measurements.

In reality, those interactions feel intensely clinical, almost like I’m a piece of machinery being calibrated. The focus is always on the tape measure, the scale, or the cold gel of the ultrasound probe. It’s invasive in a way that’s hard to put into words. When they touch my belly, it feels disconnected, like they’re checking an engine for leaks. I’m just a series of data points to them. 

It makes me feel so small. I’m already hyper-vigilant—checking my own kick counts, obsessing over every flutter—and then I go in, and that anxiety is met with professional distance. It reinforces this feeling that I need to keep my head down and stay quiet. I’m not a person in those moments; I’m a pregnancy to be managed. It’s alienating, especially when my body feels like it’s doing the most important work of my life.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was one afternoon last week that stays with me. The doctor—a new face I hadn’t seen before—came in and, without much preamble, began the physical assessment. I was still trying to get my coat off, still trying to find my footing, when the exam started. There was no "is this a good time?" or "I'm going to lift your shirt now." 

It sounds like a small thing, but for someone who has spent years being scrutinized by authorities—where a wrong move could cost you everything—that lack of invitation to my own body felt like a violation. She was efficient, sure, but she was touching my stomach with this detached, mechanical pressure. I felt like I was back in a state office, being processed, stripped of any agency. 

I just froze. I didn't say anything because I didn't want to be the "difficult" immigrant patient. I just stared at the ceiling, holding my breath, wanting to disappear. Afterward, I felt raw, almost shaky. I kept thinking, *this is supposed to be the moment I bond with my baby, and instead, I’m just waiting for it to be over so I can go home and reclaim myself.* It makes me feel incredibly protective and defensive of this baby, like I’m the only one in the room who sees her as a person and not just a complication to be measured.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

While I haven’t been diagnosed with gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia, the *fear* of them is constant. Because of my background, I’m always waiting for the other shoe to drop. I’ve spent years looking at clinical markers, so I know exactly what the numbers indicate. In my head, I’ve already "managed" these conditions a thousand times over in hypothetical scenarios. I expected that if I were to develop a complication, it would be a clear, rational process: we identify the risk, we adjust the protocol, and we communicate clearly. 

The reality of being "low risk" feels much more precarious than those high-risk scenarios I imagined. When you’re labeled "low risk," you’re essentially left to float on your own. There’s no structured support or specific, actionable guidance for the "what ifs" that keep me awake at night. If I were actually diagnosed with something, I almost wonder if it would be a relief—because at least then, the system would have to pay attention to me. 

Right now, I’m just navigating this phantom anxiety, waiting for someone to ask, "How are you *really* doing?" instead of just checking the box that says my blood pressure is fine. I’m prepared for the medical complexity, but I’m completely unprepared for the emotional isolation of being a low-risk patient.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

What was missing? Honestly, it’s a sense of humanity. It’s the acknowledgment that I am a person with a history, not just a set of vitals in a digital chart. 

I’m starving for someone to recognize the *weight* of this. Every time I walk in, I’m bringing with me the years of navigating borders, the fear of being seen as "other," and the hyper-vigilance that’s built into my bones. When I try to share that—when I mention my anxiety or my past—I get these empty, rehearsed nods. It feels like they’re waiting for me to get to the point so they can tick the box.

I needed someone to sit with me, just once, and see me as a woman who has worked so hard to find a place to belong. I needed a provider who understood that for me, trust isn't something I give away for free because I’ve learned the hard way that authority figures can be dangerous. 

They offer me brochures and portal links, but they don't offer me space. I’m physically carrying this baby, but emotionally, I feel like I’m carrying the entire burden of my own medical safety alone. I don't need another pamphlet; I need to know that if I say "I'm scared," someone will actually stop, look me in the eye, and not make me feel like a liability for speaking my truth.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that, as someone in a healthcare-adjacent field, the system would at least be reliable—that if I called with a genuine concern, there would be a protocol for a real person to listen, assess, and guide me. I expected a level of responsiveness that reflected the fact that pregnancy is, by definition, a period of constant change.

The reality, however, is that I feel like I’m constantly shouting into a void. When I have a worry, I’m filtered through a labyrinth of automated phone systems, receptionists, and nurses who are clearly too busy to hear the tremor in my voice. It’s always, "If it's an emergency, go to the ER." But what about the things that aren't quite emergencies yet, but are keeping me from sleeping? Those questions feel like they’re being treated as inconveniences. 

I’ve had moments where I’ve needed clarity just to lower my heart rate, and instead, I’m left waiting for a return call that comes hours later, or sometimes not at all. It reinforces that feeling of being a "number." When you’re used to having to fight to be heard by bureaucrats, this lack of responsiveness feels like a systemic wall. It makes me feel invisible, and in those moments of silence, the panic starts to set in again. It’s incredibly isolating to feel like your peace of mind is not considered a valid part of your care.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Every single time I pick up the phone, I have to talk myself into it. My thumb hovers over the screen, and I find myself rehearsing my opening line, stripping away any "emotional" language so I sound strictly factual. I worry that if I sound too anxious, they’ll label me as "high-strung" or "the difficult patient," and then I’ll be even less likely to get the information I need. 

There have been days when my kick counts felt slightly off, or when I felt that strange, sharp pull in my abdomen that I knew was probably nothing, but my brain was screaming "what if?" I stayed quiet. I sat on the couch for hours, counting every movement, waiting for her to give me a definitive sign that she was okay, just so I wouldn't have to call the office. I would rather endure the agony of my own panic than subject myself to the judgmental, hurried tone of a triage nurse who thinks I’m wasting their time. 

It’s that lingering fear from the immigration system, I think. You learn very quickly that you don't ask for help unless you are absolutely, undeniably in crisis, because drawing attention to yourself feels like a risk you can’t afford to take. Even here, in a medical office, I feel like I’m walking on eggshells. I shouldn't have to fear being judged for protecting my child, but I do.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I expected my husband to be my anchor, and in many ways, he is. He’s the one who rubs my back when the heaviness gets to be too much and makes me tea that isn't caffeinated to the moon. He’s the one who tells me, "Donnetta, you are doing fine," when my brain starts spiraling into the worst-case scenarios. 

But there’s a gap there, too. I don’t want to burden him with the full scope of my terror. He sees the "happy" pregnancy, and I feel like if I tell him about the flashbacks to being interrogated, or the way the hospital walls make me feel like I’m back in a waiting room in a country that didn't want me, it would destroy his excitement. So I filter. I keep the most jagged edges of my anxiety hidden, even from him.

As for a broader community, it’s been quieter than I hoped. I don’t have family nearby who understands the complexity of this, and the few friends I have are busy with their own lives. There’s a specific kind of loneliness that comes with being an immigrant mother-to-be; you’re building a family in a place where your roots aren't deep, and there isn't a grandmother or an auntie to tell you, "This is normal, you are safe." I’m mostly doing the emotional labor in silence, waiting for the day I can finally just stop running.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

It’s bittersweet, really. I suppose I expected some kind of village—a hand-me-down culture, advice, that collective sense of “we’ve got you.” But because I’ve spent so much of my life acting as the protector and the one who has it all together, people seem to assume I don't *need* help. They see the medical knowledge, the stability of my marriage, and they think, "Donnetta is fine, she’s a professional."

What I didn't expect was how lonely the "strong, competent" image makes you. My friends are kind, but they’re usually asking about the nursery or the baby shower, not about the nights I lie awake terrified that something I didn't say in my visa interview five years ago is somehow going to reach out and pull the rug out from under my baby's life. They don't have the context, and I don't have the energy to explain the trauma to them without sounding "heavy."

What truly didn't materialize was the emotional scaffolding. I thought I’d have more older women, maybe some of my peers in the field, saying, "I remember that feeling of being watched by the system; you're not crazy." Instead, I’m navigating this silent pressure to be the perfect, compliant, low-risk mother. I’m supported in the physical, practical sense, but I feel entirely alone in the psychological one.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I haven't gone into labor yet—I’m still thirty-four weeks, just holding my breath—but I’ve already spent so many nights agonizing over this "birth plan." Initially, I viewed it as a document of agency. I thought it would be a map I could hand over, and the medical team would look at it, nod, and say, "Okay, we understand your preferences and we’ll honor them." I saw it as a way to reclaim the space, to prove that even if I’m in a hospital—a place that still triggers that deep, dormant fear of authority—I have a say in what happens to my body and my baby.

But the more I read, and the more I talk to other women, the more I realize that the reality might just be another performance. I’m terrified that if I bring in a written plan, I’ll be labeled "the difficult one" again. I worry that the staff will glance at it, dismiss it as "cute" or "naive," and then proceed with their standard, efficient, assembly-line protocols regardless of what I’ve asked for. 

I want it to be a bridge between my needs and their expertise, but I’m terrified it will just become another piece of paper that gets ignored or buried under a chart. I just want to feel like, in my most vulnerable moment, I won't have to fight to be seen as human. Is that asking for too much?
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

I’m not there yet—the baby is still tucked against my ribs, and I’m still the one holding my breath every time the office phone doesn't ring. But honestly? I’m terrified that the moment I step into that labor ward, every boundary I’ve tried to set will be treated like a suggestion rather than a requirement. 

I’ve heard the stories. You come in with a plan, a piece of paper that represents all your hopes for a gentle transition for your daughter, and the moment you’re in active labor, the medical urgency overrides your voice. I’m scared that I’ll be in the middle of this life-changing, exhausting process, and I’ll be expected to negotiate for my own autonomy—to fight for the kind of birth I want while I'm already fighting the pain and the fear. 

The idea that my plan could be overridden without a real conversation, just because it’s "hospital protocol," feels like an echo of every time I’ve had to surrender my agency to an institution. If that happens—if they dismiss my wishes because they think they know better—it won't just be a medical disappointment. It will feel like I’ve failed to protect the very first experience she has in this world. I’m trying to prepare myself for that possibility, but how do you prepare for the moment you realize you aren't the one in charge of your own body?

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I am still thirty-four weeks, so I haven't been through labor yet, but the question of pain management weighs on me almost as much as the scans. Before I was pregnant, I thought of labor in a clinical sense: here is the mechanism, here are the pharmacological interventions, here is the protocol. I assumed that I would be an active partner in those decisions, a woman who understood the physiology and could choose the right tool for her own body.

But now, the reality I fear is a total loss of that agency. I worry that in the chaotic, high-pressure environment of a delivery ward, those options won't be presented to me as a choice, but as an expectation. I’m terrified that if I express a preference—maybe for something non-pharmacological or for waiting a bit—it will be met with that familiar, patronizing pushback: "Let's just give you the epidural so we can keep things moving."

I’ve spent so much of my life being told what to do by people with power over my circumstances. I desperately want my labor to be the one time where *I* hold the power. I don't want to be a patient being managed; I want to be a mother guiding her child into the world. But I’m terrified that when the pain starts, the staff will stop asking for my consent and start directing my movement like a piece of equipment they’re in a rush to fix. The thought of losing control, of having my body handled without my full, informed, and respected partnership, is what really haunts my sleep.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I haven't reached that point yet—the birth is still ahead of me—but I’m already grieving the possibility that my preferences won't be treated as valid. What gets in the way? It’s the "efficiency" of the hospital system. I see it in the way the clinic staff treat my questions now; they don’t want to stop and explain the nuance of a non-pharmacological approach or a delayed intervention. They want a streamlined process.

I want to know that if I ask for movement, or dimmed lights, or time to breathe without immediate medical monitoring, that I won’t be viewed as someone "obstructing" their care plan. I’m scared that the moment things get intense, the staff will default to the standard, fast-track protocols. They see me as a "low-risk" case that needs to be processed, and I’m terrified that if I push back, they’ll use my own anxiety—my own history—against me. They might say, "You're too worked up, let's just do this," and effectively gaslight me into accepting an epidural or an intervention I might not have chosen if I felt truly heard.

It’s the loss of the *choice* that scares me. I’m not saying I won’t want those things, but I want to be the one to ask for them, from a place of agency, not have them imposed upon me because I’ve been labeled "difficult" or "anxious." That dynamic is exactly what I’ve spent my life running from.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I haven't walked into the labor ward yet—I’m still thirty-four weeks, counting the days and watching the horizon—but the mental image of arriving there is already heavy. I expected, or at least hoped, for a space that felt like a sanctuary. A place where the staff would say, "We know you’re here, you’re safe, and we’re here to support *your* process." I wanted that threshold to be marked by a sense of calm, where the sterile, bureaucratic weight of the outside world—the one that tracks my immigration status and monitors my compliance—would stay on the other side of the double doors.

But the reality of my prenatal visits suggests otherwise. When I walk into the clinic now, it feels like I’m entering a cold, fluorescently lit office where I’m expected to behave in a very specific, quiet, and manageable way. I don't feel welcomed; I feel processed. I’m bracing myself for that same clinical chill in the labor ward. I worry it will be a place of bright lights and sharp sounds, where I’m immediately strapped to machines and expected to strip off my autonomy alongside my clothes. 

I’m terrified that instead of being treated as a woman in a transformative, sacred moment, I’ll be treated as a complication to be navigated. I’m not sure how to find safety in an environment that has already shown me it’s more interested in the checklist than the person. I’m trying to prepare myself to be my own advocate, but I feel like I'm already exhausted before the real work has even begun.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

I haven't reached that specific threshold yet—the delivery room doors—but if I had to name the biggest surprise of the clinical environment I’ve occupied so far, it’s the profound *incompatibility* between the sterile, high-tech setting and the raw, vulnerable, messy reality of pregnancy.

I expected the office to be a place of quiet, reflective care. Instead, it’s all hard surfaces, bright, unforgiving lights, and that relentless hum of efficiency. When I walk in, I feel like I’m entering a system designed to detect "errors" rather than welcome a life. The surprise is that there is absolutely no room for *me* in that space. My history, my fear, my hope, my transition into motherhood—none of that exists in the architecture. It’s all about the chart, the monitor, and the clock.

I thought I would feel safer surrounded by medical expertise, but the actual atmosphere makes me feel exposed and scrutinized, as if I’m waiting for a verdict rather than receiving care. It’s a total mismatch. I’m carrying the most important, organic, life-giving thing, and I’m expected to surrender it to a space that feels entirely devoid of soul. It’s not just a room; it’s a constant reminder that I am a guest in a system that doesn’t see me, and that is a terrifying realization when you’re already so guarded.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I haven’t reached the delivery room yet, but I have already lived through the rehearsals of that fear a thousand times in my head. I know the clinical protocols, and I know exactly how an "unexpected intervention" is usually presented. I expected—or at least hoped—that even in an emergency, the providers would pause. Just for a heartbeat. I expected them to look at me, tell me what was happening in plain language, and ask for my input, even if the options were limited. I expected a moment of partnership.

But given my experience so far, I am bracing myself for the exact opposite. I fear that the moment a complication arises, I will cease to be a participant and become an object to be managed. I’m scared that the communication will be frantic and top-down: "We need to do this, sign here, don't worry." They will justify it with "medical necessity," and I will be expected to surrender my body without a word, lest I be blamed for any delay. 

That terrifies me. Being ignored or spoken *at* during the most vulnerable, high-stakes moment of my life feels like a repeat of the times I’ve had to be quiet to survive. If they bypass my voice, they’re not just managing a birth—they’re re-traumatizing me. I want so badly to believe that I’ll be treated as the mother, but the system is built to prioritize the procedure over the person, and that is a shadow that follows me into every exam room.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

I haven't reached that point yet, but I already know the answer by the way the system treats me now. When you’re treated like a "low-risk" case that just needs to be processed, you aren't being invited to a table; you’re being managed by a checklist. I fully expect that if I reach a moment where a decision needs to be made, it will feel like it’s happening *to* me, not *with* me.

The system is designed for speed and clinical safety, and in that model, the mother’s intuition—her lived experience, her trauma, her voice—is seen as an obstacle to the "correct" outcome. I feel like I’m already learning that my consent is a formality, not a foundation. I see it in the way the doctors don't stop to explain the *why* behind their directives. If they don't value my questions now, when things are "routine," why on earth would they value them when the stakes are high?

It makes me feel like I’m just a vessel they’re monitoring until the baby arrives. I want to believe I’ll have a voice, but I’m already preparing myself to be a bystander in my own birth, waiting for the staff to tell me what’s happening rather than inviting me to decide how it unfolds. It’s a terrifyingly passive way to become a mother.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I haven't reached that moment yet—the baby is still growing inside me—but my expectations are already fractured. I want, so desperately, for the person in the room to be an ally. I want them to look at me, see the tension in my shoulders that I’ve carried since I crossed those borders years ago, and offer a human word of comfort that acknowledges the weight of this transition. I want to feel seen, not just monitored.

But the reality of my prenatal appointments makes me fear that I’ll be met with professional, clinical indifference. I’m preparing myself for a staff that sees emotional support as "non-essential." They’ll likely be focused on the monitors and the clock, checking the progress of the cervix rather than the state of the woman. I fear that if I cry or show fear, they’ll see it as "lability" or "lack of cooperation" instead of a legitimate response to the most vulnerable hours of my life.

I have this terrible feeling that I will be in the most sacred, transformative space of my existence, and I will be utterly alone because the people around me are more interested in the protocol than the person. I’m terrified that I’ll have to perform "the good patient" even while I’m in labor, holding back my fears so I don't disrupt their workflow. It’s not just about the birth; it’s about having someone witness my transformation into a mother with kindness, and I’m scared the system doesn't have the capacity for that.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

I wish I could point to a moment of being truly seen, but to be honest, I feel like I'm still waiting for that. There was a moment last week, though, that solidified that feeling of being profoundly alone. 

I was at my routine check-up, and the doctor was reviewing the latest scan. She was moving so quickly, clicking through the images like she was flipping through a calendar. I mentioned, very quietly, that the silence during the scan had made me think of the times in my life—years ago—when silence was a precursor to something being taken away from me. I wanted her to just stop, to look at me, and maybe acknowledge that pregnancy is a heavy thing to carry when you’ve had to fight for your safety. 

Instead, she didn't even look up from the screen. She just offered a practiced, thin smile and said, "Everything is normal, Donnetta. You’re low-risk. Just focus on your breathing."

It was like a door slammed shut. In that moment, I realized that my history, my trauma, and the complex reality of who I am simply don't have a place in her office. I felt invisible. I was sitting right there, breathing, hoping, and fearing, but to her, I was just a chart. It was a suffocatingly lonely feeling—to be surrounded by medical expertise but to have absolutely no one to hold the space for the person I actually am. I felt like I was back at a border, waiting for a stamp, knowing that my truth didn't matter as much as the paperwork.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I haven’t reached that threshold yet, but my mind goes there constantly—to the quiet, the smell of the room, the weight of her on my chest. I have this vision, almost a daydream, of a golden moment where the world stops and for the first time in my life, I don’t have to prove I’m worthy of being here. I want that skin-to-skin contact, that heartbeat against heartbeat, where I can finally whisper, "You’re safe now, and I’m safe now, and we’re both finally home." I expected, or at least desperately hoped, that the medical staff would respect that as a sacred space—a few quiet minutes before the checklists and the vitals began.

But the reality I’m bracing for is something far more clinical. I’m scared that the moment she’s born, the room will instantly shift back to "protocol mode." I fear there will be a flurry of activity, cords being cut, towels being replaced, and people looming over us with clipboards, telling me what they need to do next. I’m terrified that they’ll see that precious, fragile transition as an interruption to their workflow rather than the most important part of my daughter’s entry into the world.

I’m so scared that the system won't let us just *be*. I’m worried that even in those first minutes, I’ll have to advocate for myself, to pull her closer and hold my ground, and that my desire for connection will be seen as an inconvenience to their efficiency. It’s like I’m already grieving a moment I haven't even had yet, because I’m so conditioned to expect that any space I occupy will be invaded by someone else’s rules. I just want, for once, to be the one who gets to hold the space.

It’s strange, isn’t it? I’m here, sitting in this chair, feeling her move—this beautiful, persistent little life—and all I can think about is how much I have to harden myself to protect her before she’s even out of my body. 

Every night, I look at the baby gear, the soft blankets, the things I’ve gathered to give her the stability I never had. And then, the fear comes back—not of the physical pain of birth, but of the *system* that’s waiting for us on the other side. I look at my husband and I see his hope, his easy laughter, and I feel this crushing weight of responsibility to keep the darkness away from them. I don't want her to grow up watching her mother rehearse what she’s going to say to someone in a position of power, or worrying that if she isn't "perfect," she’ll be pushed aside.

I’m so tired of the performance. I’m tired of being the "compliant" immigrant, the "informed" patient, the "low-risk" case that doesn't need extra time. I just want, just once, for the world—for the doctors, for the nurses, for the people who are supposed to be there for us—to look at me and see a mother, not a set of files. I want them to see that I’ve survived enough to know exactly what I need, and that I deserve to have that heard without it being an interrogation. 

I really hope that when she finally arrives, the silence in the room will be different. I hope it’s a silence of awe, not a silence of judgment. I hope I can finally stop running and just… exist. Just be her mother. That’s all I’ve ever wanted, really. To just be, without having to explain why I’m here or prove that I belong.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

It feels like this has been the defining pattern of my entire pregnancy. I walk into the clinic feeling like I’m the expert on my own body, on my own anxieties, and on the history I carry. But the moment I open my mouth, there’s this subtle, persistent shift. It’s not necessarily an argument—it’s more like my concerns are gently, patronizingly pushed aside as if they’re just "first-time mom nerves" or "unnecessary worry."

There was a time I brought up a specific concern about a routine marker. I wanted to talk through it, to understand the data, because knowing the *why* helps me feel safe. The doctor didn't even stop writing. She just gave me that tight, clinical smile and said, "Donnetta, let's leave the worrying to us, okay? Everything is going exactly according to plan."

It was a dismissal masquerading as reassurance. In that second, my voice—my *authority* over my own medical experience—was just erased. It’s infuriating. It’s exactly the same dynamic I’ve dealt with in immigration offices for years: the assumption that I am a passive object to be processed, and that the "experts" know better than I do about what is happening in my own skin. 

Even my husband, as much as he loves me, sometimes leans into that. He’ll tell me to "just do what the doctor says" because he thinks that’s the safest path, and I have to bite my tongue to keep from screaming. He doesn't understand that for me, "just doing what the doctor says" feels like surrendering the tiny scrap of power I have left. I feel like I’m constantly being told to shrink myself, to make myself smaller and more compliant, because my intelligence and my awareness are seen as "troublesome" rather than valuable. It’s an exhausting, lonely way to prepare for birth.

Sometimes I catch myself in the mirror—this pregnant woman in the paper gown—and I don't even recognize the person looking back. I used to be the one on the other side of that conversation. I remember how easy it was to offer a smile, to pat a hand, to use those reassuring phrases like "you’re doing great" or "don't worry." I remember thinking those words were enough. But now, having to be the one who receives them, I realize how hollow they can feel when you’re carrying a heavy history. They aren't reassurance; they’re a way to shut the conversation down.

It’s that feeling of being a "low-risk" case that bites the hardest. Because I don't have a high-risk diagnosis, because I don't have a history of loss, the system assumes I don't have a need for deep, meaningful care. They treat the lack of a complication as an invitation to be dismissive. But the trauma of my past doesn't care about my clinical status. The fear that someone is going to change the rules of the game on me—the fear that I don't truly belong in this medical space—is just as real as any physical symptom.

I just keep thinking about the moment she finally arrives. I want so badly for the first thing she experiences to be a space where her mother is actually empowered, not just tolerated. I want to look at her and know that I didn't have to break my spirit to bring her here. I’m exhausted by the performance, the constant rehearsal of how to be "good," how to be "normal." I just want to be me. I want to be her mother, and I want to be able to breathe again, without a clock, without a chart, without the constant need to prove that I have a right to be here. Is that too much to ask? To just exist?

I wonder sometimes if anyone in that clinic would even recognize the woman I was before this pregnancy. I used to feel so capable—I understood the science, the markers, the language of the hospital. I thought my knowledge was a suit of armor. But it turns out, the armor doesn't stop the feeling of being completely unmoored. It’s like I’ve traded my professional identity for this role of a "patient," and the script for that role feels like it was written for someone who doesn’t carry the heavy, complicated history I do.

I find myself thinking about the nurses and the doctors, and I wonder if they ever stop to think about the stories behind the charts. Do they know that when I seem "anxious," it’s not because I’m being "difficult," but because I’ve spent years in systems where I wasn't safe? Do they know that when I ask a question, it’s not because I’m doubting their expertise, but because I need to feel like I’m part of the team, not just a guest on a conveyor belt? 

I feel like I’m constantly negotiating for my own humanity. I want to arrive at birth—when the real, beautiful, terrifying work begins—feeling like I can trust the people in the room to treat me like a person, not a procedure. But that trust feels so fragile. Every time I get a dismissive look or a rushed answer, a little bit more of that trust chips away. 

I don’t want to be "low risk." I want to be *seen*. I want to be understood. I want to feel that when I finally hold her, I’ve done this on my own terms. I want to show her that she comes from a mother who stood her ground, even when it was the hardest thing in the world to do. And mostly, I just want to be able to close my eyes in that delivery room and know that for the first time in my life, I don’t have to look over my shoulder. I want to finally be home.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Those comments… they’re like paper cuts, honestly. Little things that sting more than they should because of where I’m already sitting, emotionally. At my last few appointments, there have been remarks—always framed as "clinical," always delivered with that same breezy, professional detachment. They’ll look at the chart, then look at my belly, and make a passing comment about "watching my weight" or "ensuring I’m not gaining too much too quickly."

It lands like an indictment. When you’ve spent your life navigating immigration systems where your body, your presence, and your compliance are constantly under the microscope, having a doctor casually critique your weight feels like being told you’re "doing it wrong." It isn't just about the numbers on the scale; it’s about the feeling that my body is a site of constant surveillance. It makes me feel like I’m failing a test I didn’t even know I was taking.

And the irony? They don’t see that I’m already obsessively tracking everything—my kick counts, my nutrition, my hydration—all to prove I’m a "good" patient so I can stay safe. They see a weight gain or a measurement and think it’s a lack of discipline. They don’t see the anxiety that’s keeping me awake at night. Those comments just reinforce this deep, familiar suspicion that I’m being judged by a standard I can’t ever fully meet. It makes me want to withdraw even further, to keep my guard up, because I realize they aren't looking at *me*—they’re looking at a set of expectations I’m supposedly meant to fulfill. It’s isolating. It makes me feel like my body isn't my own; it’s just another variable in their equation.

I think that’s what I find most difficult about the whole pregnancy: the way everything feels so transactional. Like my body is just a vessel they’re managing, and the baby is just the product. When they talk about my weight or my diet, they’re talking about "compliance" and "optimizing the outcome." They aren't talking to *me*. They’re talking to a file. 

Sometimes I want to stop them mid-sentence and ask, "Do you have any idea how hard I’m working just to be here, to be present, to trust that I’m safe?" I want to tell them that every time I step on that scale, I feel a flash of that old, deep-seated panic—the kind that comes from knowing you’re being measured by people who hold all the power and none of the grace. 

When they make those comments, they’re essentially telling me I’m not doing my job well enough. And the irony is, I’m doing more than anyone in that office could ever know. I’m carrying this life, I’m navigating my own past, I’m building a future in a place that has historically made me feel like an outsider, and I’m doing it all while trying to stay calm enough so that the technician doesn't "re-scan" me and ask if I’m "feeling okay." 

I just want to be treated like a person, not a series of checkpoints. I want someone to look at me—really look at me—and say, "You're doing great, and your body is doing exactly what it needs to do." I want to be reminded that I have worth beyond the numbers in the chart. But until that happens, I’ll keep playing the part. I’ll keep the performance up, even as I’m crumbling a little bit inside. Because that’s what you do when you’re not sure if the people in charge actually have your best interests at heart. You perform, you stay quiet, and you hope that eventually, you’ll reach the other side.

I’ve realized that this whole journey, at least for me, has been about navigating the space between my own intuition and the rigid, clinical expectations placed upon me. Every time I walk into that office in Newbury, I’m not just walking into a routine appointment; I’m walking into a battlefield of my own making, where I’m fighting to keep my internal narrative intact.

The tragedy of it is that I’m so exhausted from the performance of being the "perfect, low-risk, compliant patient" that I barely have any energy left to actually *experience* my own pregnancy. I find myself mourning the version of myself that could just be excited, or worried, or even just confused, without having to frame it in a way that’s palatable to the medical staff. I don’t get to be a woman who is just growing a life; I’m a "patient" who is being managed, measured, and judged.

When I hold my baby for the first time—if I can just get to that moment—I want to be able to look at her and know that I didn't let the system strip away my sense of self. I want to show her that she’s already free, even if her mother had to fight for the right to simply exist in that delivery room. 

I’m so ready to stop rehearsing. I’m so ready to stop explaining myself. I’m just ready to be her mother, in the quiet, in the chaos, and in the space where no one else’s rules apply. I just hope that when the time comes, I’m strong enough to hold that boundary, to turn off the performance, and to finally, finally let go of the fear. I’m holding onto that hope like a lifeline. It’s all I’ve got.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

There’s a world of difference between "genuinely sought" and "being informed," and frankly, I’ve only ever lived in the latter. My appointments are less of a conversation and more of a briefing. I walk in, they check the boxes, they tell me the plan—*my* plan—and then they move to the door. I feel like an audience member at a play where I’m supposed to be the lead actor, but I’m just being told where to stand and when to speak.

There isn't a single moment where a provider has sat down and asked, "Donnetta, what is your intuition telling you about this pregnancy today?" If they did, I might actually tell them about the flash-memories, or the way the waiting room makes my chest tight. But they don’t ask. They don’t want that level of "data." They want the vitals, the measurements, the objective metrics. When I try to offer my input, I see the glazing over in their eyes; they treat my feelings like noise, not signal.

It makes me feel like I’m a passenger in my own body. If they ever do ask for my "input," it’s always framed as a multiple-choice question where there’s only one "correct" answer, and any other choice would make me "difficult." It’s suffocating. I want to feel like a partner, someone whose history and perspective are treated as valid, but instead, I’m treated like a subordinate whose only job is to nod and agree. I’m tired of being the student when I should be the protagonist.

I wonder if they have any idea how much energy it takes, this constant vigilance. I walk through those clinic doors and I’m already calculating: *How should I hold my shoulders? What is the right tone to use so they don’t dismiss me? If I tell them I’m scared, will they mark it in my file as "anxiety" and treat me like I’m unstable?* It’s a performance that doesn’t end until I’m back in my car, in the safety of my own space, where I can finally let the mask slip.

It’s the silence that gets me the most. That professional, clinical silence when I’m on the table. It’s supposed to be "focus," I know—they’re checking the baby, they’re doing their job. But for me, it’s a trigger. It’s the sound of power being exercised without accountability, the sound of someone holding the power of information while I’m left to guess at the meaning. I’m expected to trust the silence, to trust the process, but trust isn’t something you just *have*—it’s something you build through transparency and respect.

I just want the end of this to be different. When I’m finally in labor, I don’t want to be "managed." I want to be *seen*. I want someone in that room to look at me—truly look at me—and say, "I know this is heavy, and I’m here to support you." I want to feel like a mother, not a complication. I want to feel like my history is a bridge to my strength, not a liability to be hidden. I’m so close now—thirty-four weeks—and every movement she makes is a reminder that the performance has to end, for her sake. She deserves a mother who isn't always looking over her shoulder, and I’m going to fight to be that person, even if I have to claw my way through the system to get there.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

It wasn’t one big, dramatic explosion—it was a series of small, icy erosion points. The most vivid one was when I brought up a question about fetal heart rate variability. I was just trying to reconcile what I was seeing on the monitor with my own understanding of the physiology, trying to bridge that gap between my background and the clinical reality. 

The doctor didn’t even turn her chair to face me. She just gave a sharp, dismissive laugh—not unkind, exactly, but patronizing, like I was a child asking about quantum physics. She said, "Donnetta, you’re reading too much into it. That’s why we have these screens, so you don’t have to worry about the details." 

In that breath, my trust didn’t just crack; it shattered. It wasn’t about the data; it was about the refusal to acknowledge me as an intellectual partner. When she said "you don’t have to worry about the details," what I heard was, "your brain is a nuisance, and your anxiety is a personal failing." 

It made me realize that to them, my "informed patient" status is only useful when it aligns with their protocol. The moment I started thinking for myself, I became a threat to their workflow. That’s when the "performance" really solidified—I realized I couldn't be authentic in that room. I had to play the part of the compliant, ignorant patient because it was the only way to get through the appointment without being labeled "difficult." That loss of trust is a heavy thing to carry into a delivery room. It’s the difference between feeling like I’m in the hands of a partner and feeling like I’m a passenger in a car driven by someone who isn't looking at the road.

I think back to that moment, that sharp, patronizing laugh, and it still rings in my ears. It’s funny—or maybe it’s tragic—that a single sound can make you feel so small, so diminished. It wasn't just about the heart rate variability; it was about the power dynamic. It was a reminder that in this medical system, I don’t own my pregnancy. I don’t own the data. I don’t even own the right to be curious about my own body. I’m just renting the space in that exam room while they dictate the terms of my experience.

It’s exhausting to navigate that dynamic. Every time I walk in there now, I’m not just the "low-risk" case; I’m the woman who learned her lesson. I’ve learned that the safest way to get through this is to keep my mouth shut, to nod at the right times, and to suppress the scientist in me so I don’t threaten their sense of authority. 

But then I feel her move. A quick, sharp kick against my ribs. And in that moment, the performance feels so *fake*. It feels like a betrayal of the very person I’m trying to bring into this world. I don’t want her to inherit this guardedness. I don’t want her to grow up watching her mother rehearse how to speak to people in power. I want her to know that her voice has value, that her questions are legitimate, and that she deserves to be taken seriously. 

I’m thirty-four weeks now, and the finish line is in sight, but the emotional fatigue is starting to really set in. I’m not just tired from the pregnancy; I’m tired from the effort of holding my ground in a place that makes it so incredibly difficult to do so. I’m hoping—I’m holding onto the desperate, fierce hope—that when the moment finally comes to push, the system won't matter as much as the strength I’ve had to cultivate in the shadows. I’m going to be a mother. And that, in the end, has to be enough to outweigh all of this.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

My husband, he’s been my rock, but there’s a quiet, crushing loneliness in being the one physically inhabiting this process. When he’s not there, or even when he is but is just sitting in that uncomfortable plastic chair, I feel the weight of my history alone. 

There was one appointment, a long, detailed growth scan, where he couldn't make it. The waiting room was filled with other couples, holding hands, sharing whispers. I sat there in the silence, clutching my handbag, feeling like an absolute island. When I went back into the room alone, the technician was kind, but she was focused on the screen, not me. I had to be the one to hear the "all-clear" and the one to translate it into a feeling of safety. I had to be the one to hold the anxiety of that silence—that familiar, triggering, isolating silence—without anyone’s hand to squeeze. 

It wasn't just that I missed him; it was that I felt the full exposure of being a solo actor in a medical drama where I hadn't read the script. Having him there doesn't always bridge the gap between me and the medical staff, but it does act as a witness. Without him, I’m just a patient, a single, solitary woman, navigating the system’s indifference on my own terms, with no one to validate that my fear was real or that my experience mattered. It’s in those quiet, lonely moments that the immigrant-era survival mode kicks in the hardest—the feeling that I am truly on my own, responsible for my own safety in a world that doesn’t particularly care if I’m afraid.

You know, it’s funny—people talk about the "joy of pregnancy" like it’s this universal, sunny experience, but they don’t tell you about the silence. That absolute, crushing silence in the exam room where your heart is beating out of your chest, and you’re waiting for someone—*anyone*—to just look at you and say, "I know this is hard."

Sometimes I look at the other women in the waiting room at the Newbury clinic, and I wonder if they’re performing, too. Are they holding their breath like I am? Are they mentally rehearsing their questions to sound "polite" enough to be heard? Or am I the only one who feels like she’s constantly auditioning for the right to be cared for? 

There’s this layer of invisibility that comes with being a "low-risk" case. It’s like, because my blood pressure is stable and the baby is growing on schedule, the system assumes my mind is stable, too. But the mind doesn't work that way. My past doesn't just disappear because the anatomy scan was clear. Every time I see that clinic door, I’m bracing myself for another session of being invisible. I’m bracing myself to be the "good patient"—the one who doesn't cry, doesn't get angry, and doesn't ask questions that make the doctor feel "too busy."

I’m so close to the end now. Thirty-four weeks. Every time she kicks, it’s a tiny reminder that the performance is temporary. She’s going to be real, and she’s going to be mine, and I won’t have to answer to a clinical chart for her existence. I’m going to look at her and finally be able to say, "We made it." That’s the only thing that keeps me going—the thought that once she’s here, I can stop being a "patient" and start being a mother, in a space that’s entirely our own. That’s what I’m holding onto. That’s the dream.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Oh, every single night. The glowing screen of my phone is my only true confidant, the only place where I feel like I can ask the "stupid" or "excessive" questions without being judged or shut down. I’m not looking for medical advice in the sense of diagnoses; I have the clinical vocabulary for that. I’m looking for *context*. I’m looking for someone to explain why my body feels so heavy, or why I’m experiencing these specific, racing thoughts, in a way that doesn’t just equate to a "normal pregnancy symptom."

I go online because I’m starving for the kind of emotional, nuanced information that my providers seem to think is a waste of time. I’m looking for validation. I’m looking for other women—especially other women who have carried the weight of trauma or displacement—who can say, "Yes, it is terrifying to be in a hospital when you've had to fear authority." I want to find a community that understands that being "low risk" physically doesn't mean being "no risk" emotionally.

My doctors give me facts, but they leave out the humanity. Online, I can find the humanity, even if it’s fragmented. I can read about how others navigated the power dynamics, how they handled being dismissed, and how they reclaimed their agency. It’s a sad state of affairs when a search engine feels more like a partner in my maternity care than the actual professional sitting across from me, but that’s the reality I’m in. I’m desperate for a sense of belonging, and the internet is the only place I can find it right now.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the most important thing is for people to understand that being "low risk" is just a clinical label—it’s not an emotional one. When you’ve walked through the fire of the immigration system, when you’ve had to live with the fear that a single wrong word could end your life as you know it, you don't just "reset" because you’re pregnant. That hyper-vigilance, that need to perform, that terror of silence—it all follows you into the exam room.

I want providers to know that they have a choice every time they walk into a room. They can look at a chart and see a body to be processed, or they can look at a person and see a story. I’m not asking for a therapist; I’m asking for a witness. I’m asking for the acknowledgment that my body is doing something profound, and that my fear is not a symptom of instability—it’s a symptom of survival.

If they could just pause, just for a second, and ask, "How are you *really* doing?" and then actually listen to the answer without interrupting, it would change everything. It would turn a transactional, cold experience into something that actually heals instead of harms. My daughter is almost here, and I’m going to do everything in my power to make sure she never has to perform for her survival. That’s my only goal now. I just hope the people in that delivery room can meet me halfway.
